coredata.dc:description: The energy crisis and the availability of pure water have become a global concern; thus, a cost-effective and environment-friendly hybrid bifunctional catalyst is required that can serve as photocatalyst and electrocatalyst. In this study, we have synthesized a new nanocomposite, Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS. This composite was synthesized via a facile method, and its structural, morphological, and chemical properties were systematically analyzed using FTIR analysis, XRD, and SEM with EDX techniques. Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS composite displayed remarkable electrochemical stability and catalytic activity in water splitting applications, due to a lower bandwidth of 3.2 eV, with an oxygen evolution reaction (OER) onset potential of 1.33 V and a favorable Tafel slope of 82.7 mVdec- 1, demonstrating its potential for high-performance electrocatalytic applications. Most importantly, the composite also exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance, achieving a degradation efficiency of 95.2% for Malachite Green (MG) dye under sunlight exposure. The photocatalytic degradation followed pseudo-first-order kinetics, highlighting the significant enhancement in catalytic performance due to the synergistic effect of ZIF-8@CS, which provides multiple active sites and facilitates efficient electron transfer. These results suggest that Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS is a capable candidate for both environmental remediation and energy conversion technologies.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The energy crisis and the availability of pure water have become a global concern; thus, a cost-effective and environment-friendly hybrid bifunctional catalyst is required that can serve as photocatalyst and electrocatalyst. In this study, we have synthesized a new nanocomposite, Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS. This composite was synthesized via a facile method, and its structural, morphological, and chemical properties were systematically analyzed using FTIR analysis, XRD, and SEM with EDX techniques. Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS composite displayed remarkable electrochemical stability and catalytic activity in water splitting applications, due to a lower bandwidth of 3.2 eV, with an oxygen evolution reaction (OER) onset potential of 1.33 V and a favorable Tafel slope of 82.7 mVdec- 1, demonstrating its potential for high-performance electrocatalytic applications. Most importantly, the composite also exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance, achieving a degradation efficiency of 95.2% for Malachite Green (MG) dye under sunlight exposure. The photocatalytic degradation followed pseudo-first-order kinetics, highlighting the significant enhancement in catalytic performance due to the synergistic effect of ZIF-8@CS, which provides multiple active sites and facilitates efficient electron transfer. These results suggest that Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS is a capable candidate for both environmental remediation and energy conversion technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental remediation procedures such as water oxidation reactions (OER) and photocatalysis have proven essential in solving the worldwide crisis with water purification and renewable energy. Extreme water pollution, due to rapid urbanization and industry, is causing serious health and environmental issues [1]. Water oxidation reactions (OER), one of several strategies, is proven to be beneficial in producing sustainable energy and is a vital step in renewable technologies, like metal-air batteries and environmental cleaning systems. Nevertheless, development of efficient electrocatalysts is vital tool for OER efficiency. The exceptional catalytic efficacy of noble-metal-based catalysts, such as iridium (Ir) and platinum (Pt), makes them operative for OER. Resulting a growing interest in creating affordable, dual-purpose metal-based electrocatalysts. Despite their efficiency, practical uses of them are severely limited due to high cost and low availability [2–5]. Carbon based nanocomposites and transition metal oxides (TMOs) have become widespread alternatives due to their cost-effective nature and high catalytic activity [5,6]. Among these, Fe/Ni-based oxides have proved to have extraordinary potential for OER owing to their exceptional stability and catalytic efficacy. Unfortunately, their relatively low oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activity restricts their applications in energy storage and conversion devices [7,8]. To overcome this issue, carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), having high conductivity and stability, are often mixed with Fe/Ni-based oxides [9,10]. Recent investigations have proved the potential of transition metal oxides- based quantum dots as catalysts for both ORR and OER. Owing to their small size, high electron-transferring ability, and large surface area, these catalysts have provided new paths to develop efficient electrocatalysts [11,12]. Despite these studies, noteworthy research gap still exists in developing efficient, cost-effective, and lifelong electrocatalysts for simultaneous ORR and OER. Therefore, there is an urgent need to explore novel material combinations and innovative synthesis approaches to overcome these limitations.

Toxic water-soluble dyes are significant contaminants, threatening aquatic ecosystems and human health [13]. The release of toxic substances such as heavy metal ions, fertilizers, phenols, and synthetic dyes into water resources [14,15]. Textile industry effluents, rich in synthetic dyes, significantly pollute water and threaten natural ecosystems [16,17]. Despite the Earth’s vast coverage of water- approximately 70 % of its surface - only a meager 2.5 % is fresh water, of which a significant portion is inaccessible [2,3,18] to humans, predicting that by 2025, 1.8 billion people will experience water stress [4,5]. The textile industry, which accounts for two-thirds of global dyestuff production [19], has significantly contributed to water pollution, with malachite green (MG), a widely used cationic dye and antifungal agent, being a major pollutant [20,21]. The persistent use of MG poses serious health risks, including carcinogenic effects, and its high solubility and low degradability make it challenging to remove using conventional methods [22]. Therefore, it demands an urgent path to developing effective and sustainable solutions for removing such hazardous dyes from polluted water sources. Photocatalysis uses sunlight to generate electron-hole pairs in semiconductors, driving oxidation-–reduction reactions that break down organic pollutants, offering an efficient solution for water decontamination [23–26]. Numerous materials, including metal-based semiconductors and single-phase semiconductor oxides [27], have been explored as potential photocatalysts [28,29], but those are unable to provide a complete and sustainable solution [8,9,30–34].

Among the emerging materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained noteworthy attention because of their exceptional optical, electrical, and physicochemical characteristics, making them highly effective in photocatalysis [35]. Increased photocatalytic effectiveness is a result of their outstanding electron transfer characteristics, high charge carrier mobility, variable bandgap, and nanoscale size. Because of these characteristics, GQDs can function as effective photosensitizers, enhancing light absorption, charge separation, and the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), all of which are essential for solar energy conversion and environmental cleanup [11,36]. GQDs are the perfect photocatalyst because of their compact size, adjustable characteristics, and capacity to promote energy transfer. In addition to GQDs, Zeolitic Imidazolate Frameworks (ZIFs), subclass of MOFs, with their high surface area, tunable pore sizes, and remarkable porosity, have emerged as effective adsorbents for dye removal [37–39]. ZIF-8 has been thoroughly investigated in environmental remediation for the removal of dyes [40,41], adsorption of heavy metals, and photocatalytic destruction of organic contaminants [42,43]. Furthermore, the selective collection of impurities is made possible by its well-defined porosity structure, and its resilience to severe environments expands its use in actual wastewater treatment [44–49]. Charge transport, catalytic activity, and pollutant degradation efficiency may all be further improved by the synergistic combination of GQDs with ZIF-8, providing a fresh approach to the creation of cutting-edge materials for environmentally and energy-friendly applications.

In this study, Fe@N-GQDs/ZIF-8@CS nanocomposite is synthesized to combine the synergistic properties of GQDs, ZIF-8, and CS for enhancing OER and photocatalytic degradation reaction. The as-synthesized nanocomposite exhibits promising OER performance, showing Tafel slope analysis of 82.7 mVdec- 1 for OER, respectively. The photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite was evaluated by observing degradation of MG dye, achieving notable degradation of 95.2 % within 90 min under sunlight, indicating its potential as a bifunctional electrocatalyst for both energy-related and environmental remediation.

Citric acid (C₆H₈O₇, 99.9 %), urea (CH₄N₂O, 99.9 %), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO₃)₂·6H₂O, 99.9 %), 2-methylimidazole (C₄H₆N₂, 99.9 %), ferric chloride (FeCl₃, 99.9 %), acetic acid (CH₃COOH, 99.9 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 99.9 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.9 %), methanol (CH₃OH, 99.9 %), and malachite green (MG, C₂₃H₂₅ClN₂, 99.9 %). All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and were of analytical grade, eliminating the need for further purification steps. Chitosan (C₅₆H₁₀₃N₉O₃₉) was extracted from the fish scale as described in Section 2.3.

FTIR spectroscopy, identifying functional groups, was recorded from 500–4000 cm-¹ using an Alpha Bruker ATR instrument with OPUS/Mentor software at the Islamia University of Bahawalpur, Pakistan. In addition, X-ray diffraction (XRD) using PANalytical X’Pert Pro MPD, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using SEM, S4500, Hitachi, and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analyses were conducted for investigation of phase composition, crystallinity, morphology, and elemental composition. Electrochemical water splitting was evaluated using a GAMRY potentiostat/galvanostat (Reference 3000).

Step 1, GQDs were prepared [50] by mixing citric acid and urea in deionized water to form a slurry. The slurry was heated to 400 °C in furnace for 5 h and then cooled to room temperature. Product was purified by washing thrice with deionized water, dried at 80 °C for 5 h, and yielded a dark greyish-black powder. Step 2, as synthesized GQDs and ferric chloride (FeCl₃) were dispersed in deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was then magnetically stirred for 22 h at room temperature. Product was washed with deionized water to remove impurities and dried in an oven. This simple synthesis method enables scalable production of Fe-functionalized GQDs for catalysis and environmental remediation. Step 3, chitosan was extracted from fish scales through a three-step process (demineralization, deproteinization, and, deacetylation) [51]. Fish scales were treated with 2 % HCl for 20 h and then neutralized. The scales were soaked in 4 % NaOH for 20 h and neutralized to form chitin. Chitin was treated with 4 % NaOH at 60 °C for 20 h, neutralized, washed, and dried at 60 °C for 10 h to obtain chitosan.

Step 4, in-situ growth of ZIF-8 nanoparticles on chitosan was carried out using a modified procedure [52]. Chitosan (0.13 g) was dissolved in 50 mL methanol (Solution A), stirred for 150 min, and sonicated for 100 min. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (1.18 g) was dissolved in 50 mL methanol (Solution B) and gradually added to Solution A under stirring. After 15 min, 1.45 g of 2-methylimidazole dissolved in 50 mL methanol (Solution C) was introduced. The reaction mixture was stirred for 2.5 h, and the precipitate was washed with methanol and dried at 60 °C. In step 5, 70 mL of 1 % Fe@GQDs stock solution was added dropwise to 70 mL ZIF-8@CS solution in 0.1 M acetic acid and stirred for 1 hour for crosslinking, followed by 24 h of curing. The blackish product was decanted, washed thrice with deionized water, and dried at 80 °C. The schematic illustration is shown in Fig. 1 .

FTIR spectra of Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS nanocomposite was compared with precursors, Fe@GQDs and ZIF-8@CS (Fig. 2 ). The FTIR analysis revealed a broad and intense peak between 2800 and 3400 cm-¹, showing stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (OH), amine (NH), and aromatic (CH) functional groups. Characteristic peaks at 1574 and 1397 cm-¹ correspond to CC and CN stretching vibrations, respectively. A peak at 1034 cm-¹ was observed due to COC stretching vibrations [50,53]. The presence of peaks at 580 and 631 cm-¹ indicates FeO bonding, while the peak at 1643 cm-¹ corresponds to OH bending vibrations [52,54,55]. These observations confirm the successful incorporation of the functional groups in Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS composite closely matching the findings in previous reports.

XRD patterns of Fe@GQDs and the synthesized Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS composite, recorded in the 2θ range of 10° to 80°, confirm the successful integration of all individual components, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . The peak residue at 15.85° and a wide peak at 18.04° correspond to plane 002, in patterns of Fe@GQDs, showing the formation of GQDs. However, peaks at 31.05° and 35.70° are due to iron oxides [56]. The diffraction peaks GQDs were observed at 2θ = 15.84°, 18.60°, and 22.80°which are attributed to the (002), (310), (220) plane, consistent with the characteristic crystallographic reflections reported for GQDs [56]. ZIF-8 displayed prominent diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.40°, 12.70°, 13.70°, 16.40°, 18.00°, 24.50°, and 30.00°, corresponding to the (110), (211), (022), (310), (222), (233), and (244) planes, respectively, in agreement with previously reported data [57,58]. The chitosan matrix exhibited a distinct diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.00° (220), along with additional peaks at 26.3°, 40.2°, 47.2°, 49.9°, 53.6°, 64.6°, 76.76°, and 88.0°, indicative of its semi-crystalline nature [59]. Characteristic diffraction peaks confirmed the presence of iron oxide at 2θ = 31.05°,35.70°(JCPDS #62–1030), which aligns with the standard reflections for iron oxide phases [59]. The XRD results collectively validate successful synthesis of the Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS composite, demonstrating structural integrity and coexistence of all constituent components.

SEM analysis, shown in Fig. 4 (a and c), reveals that GQDs tend to aggregate due to high-temperature treatment [60]. The unique capability of GQDs to accommodate numerous functional groups and edge sites facilitates their attachment to metal oxide nanocomposites, thereby enhancing catalysts’ stability in reaction cycles [61]. Iron oxide was observed to have adopted a rod-like morphology [62], while ZIF-8 exhibited hexagonal and cubic-shaped particles, which is consistent with literature reports [63]. The SEM images of chitosan demonstrated surface disintegration, indicating reduced crystallinity, which may enhance the polymer's amorphous properties and improve its interaction with other components [64].

EDX analysis Fig. 4b, was done alongside SEM to confirm the synthesis of composite by determining its elemental composition which confirmed the presence of elemental compositions such as Carbon (C), Oxygen (O), Zinc (Zn), Iron (Fe), Calcium (Ca), and trace amounts of other elements. The presence of Sodium (Na) can be attributed to NaOH used in the neutralization of chitosan. The Calcium peak may arise from the residual chitin in the composite, alongside small amounts of Sulfur (S) and Phosphorus (P). Composite’s average particle size was 0.9 mm as estimated via particle size histogram, shown in Fig. 4(d) [65].

The optical properties of Fe@GQDs and Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS were explored using UV–Vis spectroscopy. Intermediate product and final nanocomposite were dispersed in ethanol via sonication to ensure uniform suspension, and their absorbance was subsequently measured. Band gap was determined by extrapolating Tauc plot, which represents relationship between (αhυ)² and photon energy (hυ), as illustrated in Fig. 5 . Here, α represents the absorption coefficient, and hυ is the incident photon energy. Band gap (Eg) was obtained by extrapolating the linear portion of Tauc plot to the x-axis, where (αhυ)² approaches zero. The estimated band gap values for Fe@GQDs and Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS were found to be 3.8 eV and 3.2 eV, respectively. Observed reduction in band gap after incorporation of ZIF-8 and CS suggests enhanced electronic interactions and improved charge transfer within composite, which may contribute to its superior photocatalytic performance.

Electrochemical water splitting was evaluated via cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to study OER. These experiments were conducted using a GAMRY potentiostat/galvanostat (Reference 3000). These measurements were conducted using three-electrode setup; Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS acting as working electrode, a platinum wire serving as counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl (3 M/KCl) electrode used as reference electrode. The measured potentials were converted to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale using the Eq. (1):
(1)
E RHE
=
E measured
+ 0.059 × pH +
E ref
Where Eref is the potential of the reference electrode (0.197 V vs. Ag/AgCl).

EIS was performed for the determination of resistances, including charge transfer resistance (Rct) and solution resistance (Rs). Cyclic voltammetry (CV), in an alkaline electrolyzer, revealed significant catalytic current for water splitting. This confirmed that the catalyst was not only electroactive but also tightly bound to the NF substrate. CV was recorded, over small range of potential, by varying scan rates as: 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV/s. The as per synthesized nanocomposite electrode’s OER activity was evaluated via LSV in alkaline electrolyte (6 M KOH). The kinetic parameters were analyzed using the Tafel Eq. (2):
(2)
η
= a + b . log
( j )
Where η = Overpotential (V), a = Tafel intercept, b = Tafel slope (V/dec or mV/dec), and j= Current density (mA/cm²). The overpotential (η) was determined using Eq. (3):
(3)
η
=
E applied
−
E eq
where Eapplied is the measured working potential (V) and E is the equilibrium potential for OER (1.23 V vs. RHE). A crucial factor in determining catalytic efficiency is the overpotential; more electrocatalytic activity is indicated by lower η values. The CV results, shown in Fig. 6 (a), reveal excellent catalytic efficiency of the composite. Tafel slope analysis (Fig. 6(b)), having a value of 82.7 mVdec−1 , indicates that Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS requires low overpotentials of 150 mV and 380 mV to reach current densities of 20 mA/cm² and 100 mA/cm², respectively. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 6(c)) further confirms low charge transfer resistance and high electron-hole separation efficiency of composite. Current density of 20 mA/cm² is used as a benchmark to evaluate catalytic activity in water-splitting applications. Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS exhibited a sharp onset potential of 1.33 V vs. RHE for OER, and an ultimate current density of 364.48 mA/cm² at 2.00 V vs. RHE, demonstrating its promising performance for efficient water splitting (Fig. 6(d)). Short-term stability of composite activity was investigated via step cyclic potentiometry. The results show that composite becomes stable after 2 cycles (20 s) and remains stable up to end of analysis (i.e., 100 s) as shown in Fig. 6(e). Comparison of OER study of synthesized Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS nanocomposite with previously reported work is shown in Table 1 .

At the anode, the Fe@GQDs facilitate the oxidation of water molecules through OER. The mechanism of which is shown in Fig. 6(f). Upon exposure to light or an applied potential, electron-hole pairs are generated within the Fe@GQDs. The photogenerated holes (h⁺) migrate to surface, interacting with water molecules adsorbed on the electrode. These holes act as strong oxidizing agents, initiating the oxidation process by extracting electrons from water molecules. This leads to formation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and subsequently hydroxide ions (OH⁻), which further react to form molecular oxygen (O₂) and protons (H⁺). The overall reaction progresses through multiple proton-coupled electron transfer steps, ultimately resulting in the evolution of O₂ gas at the anode. The released protons contribute to the overall electrochemical process by participating in the reduction reactions at the cathode, maintaining charge balance within the system. This reaction can be represented by Eq. (4)–(6):

Photocatalytic performance of composite was evaluated by studying degradation of Malachite Green (MG) dye, using as-synthesized nanocomposite; Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS and Fe@GQDs under sunlight, which exhibits a maximum absorption wavelength (λmax) at 618 nm. Before the photocatalytic reaction, dark adsorption experiments were performed to evaluate both the adsorption capacity and equilibrium time of the synthesized photocatalysts. Specifically, 10 mg of synthesized catalyst was dispersed in 60 mL of 20 ppm MG dye solution and stirred in dark for 15 min to achieve adsorption equilibrium. Afterward, solution was exposed to sunlight, and 3 mL of solution was extracted at 10-minute intervals (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, and 90 min) until complete degradation occurred. Degradation curves of Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS and Fe@GQDs are shown in Fig. 7 (a) and 7(b). Percentage of dye degradation was calculated using Eq. (7) and is shown in Fig. 7(c) for Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS.
(7)
%
Degradation =
[
1 −
C t
C o
]
× 100
%
where Co is the initial absorbance and Ct is the absorbance at a given time.

Percentage of MG degradation using Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS at various time intervals (10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, and 90 min) was determined to be 26.1 %, 44 %, 52.1 %, 60.1 %, 74.6 %, 85.4 %, 91.4 %, 93.1 %, and 95.2 %, respectively, while that of Fe@GQDs degrades MG only 78.36 % in 90 min, as shown in Fig. 7(d). This degradation pattern is plotted against absorbance over time, demonstrating the progress of MG dye degradation.

A kinetic study was conducted to determine degradation rate and elucidate the underlying mechanism of the photodegradation process. The data shows a pseudo-first-order kinetic model, based on the Langmuir–Hinshelwood model [69]. The higher R2 value of 0.97435 for Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS, compared to 0.80 for Fe@GQDs, confirms that the photodegradation process closely follows pseudo-first-order kinetics, indicating a more consistent and reliable reaction pathway. The calculated rate constant for Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS is 0.03 m−1 while that of Fe@GQDs is 0.01 m−1, showing a high degradation efficiency of Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS, as illustrated in Fig. 7(e) and Fig. 7(f), where plot of ln(Ct/C0) versus time is presented. Comparative photocatalytic study of Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS nanocomposite was done with previously reported nanocomposites (Table 2 ). The investigation showed a remarkably high %degradation of as-synthesized nanocomposite.

The photocatalytic degradation of MG dye by Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS likely proceeds through a mechanism involving reactive oxygen species (ROS), as outlined in Fig. 8 . The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of malachite green dye facilitated by the Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS composite involves a synergistic interaction between its components, enhancing light absorption, charge separation, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. Upon exposure to sunlight, Fe@GQDs act as efficient photocatalysts, absorbing photons and generating electron-hole pairs (e-/h⁺). The photogenerated electrons (e⁻) in the conduction band (CB) and holes (h⁺) in the valence band (VB) drive subsequent redox reactions. The electrons are transferred from Fe@GQDs to the ZIF-8@CS matrix, which acts as a stabilizing and electron-accepting platform, reducing charge recombination and enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. The electrons transferred to ZIF-8@CS interact with adsorbed oxygen (O₂) molecules, producing superoxide radicals (•O₂⁻), which can further react to form hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂).

Simultaneously, the holes (h⁺) remaining in the Fe@GQDs oxidize surface-bound water (H₂O) and hydroxide ions (OH⁻), generating additional hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are highly reactive and serve as the primary agents in the degradation of malachite green dye. They attack the aromatic rings and various functional groups of the dye molecules, breaking down complex structures into smaller, less toxic molecules such as carbon dioxide (CO₂), water (H₂O), and simple organic compounds. ZIF-8@CS not only provides a high surface area for the adsorption of dye molecules but also stabilizes Fe@GQDs, facilitating efficient electron transfer and minimizing charge recombination. This cooperative interaction between Fe@GQDs and ZIF-8@CS maximizes ROS production and enhances the overall photodegradation efficiency of the composite material. The rate constant for photodegradation was found to be 0.03 min- 1 , which suggests that the reaction follows first-order kinetics [73].

This study successfully describes the fabrication of Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS nanocomposite with enhanced photocatalytic and electro-catalytic performance, targeting wastewater treatment and water-splitting applications. The Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS composite demonstrated promising results in photocatalytic and electrocatalytic applications. FTIR analysis confirmed the presence of imidazole and iron through peaks at 1397 cm⁻¹ and 631 cm⁻¹, respectively. SEM-EDX confirmed the uniform elemental distribution, while XRD analysis verified the composite's crystallinity, with an average particle size of 0.9 mm.

Optical properties revealed that the nanocomposite has a higher band width of 3.2 eV as compared to Fe@GQDs, having a band width of 3.8 eV. Electrochemical analysis revealed excellent water-splitting performance, with an OER onset potential of 1.33 V vs. RHE and a Tafel slope of 82.7 mV/dec, indicating high catalytic efficiency. Photocatalytic degradation of Malachite Green under sunlight, using as synthesized composite, achieved 95.2 % efficiency within 90 min, which is much higher than that of Fe@GQDs, showing only 78.36 % in 90 min, following pseudo-first-order kinetics (rate constant: 0.03 min-¹, R² = 0.974). The composite's superior performance is attributed to the synergy between Fe@GQDs and the ZIF-8@CS framework, enhancing charge separation, electron transfer, and surface area. Fe and GQDs contribute conductivity and catalytic activity, while ZIF-8@CS ensures stability, reusability, and structural integrity.

In summary, the Fe@GQDs/ZIF-8@CS nanocomposite exhibits significant potential as an efficient catalyst for both dye degradation and water splitting applications, which underlines its potential for practical, sustainable environmental and energy applications.
